coredata.dc:title: Effects of functional groups on carbon dots-based AuNPs for the specific detection of mercury ions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Shaopeng
Yang, Dezhi
Li, Qiulan
Li, Jitao
Zhong, Dubo
Yang, Ye
Yang, Yaling
Wang, Hua
coredata.dc:description: The design of highly selective Raman substrates has always been the key to metal ion detection using surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS). Here, a solution volatilization method (SVM) by reducing carbon dots (CDs) was proposed to prepare highly selective gold nanoparticle (AuNPs) as Raman substrates for the specific detection of Hg2+ through SERS. Five CDs-based AuNPs were synthesized by changing the group on the precursor materials for CDs preparation and the secondary reduction with seed method. The results revealed that thiol and hydroxyl groups played a pivotal role in the detection of Hg2+. Interestingly, the influence of the chirality, number and type of the groups on the detection of Hg2+ is substantial. The DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2, prepared from dithiothreitol (DTT) containing two thiol groups and hydroxyl groups, was capable of selectively facilitating the formation of gold amalgamation (Au@HgNPs) with Hg2+, without being affected by methylmercury. The Au@HgNPs showed obviously enhanced oxidase (OXD)-like activity, which can oxidize TMB (weakly SERS-active) to ox-TMB (strongly SERS-active) with strong Raman signal. This led to a rapid and specific SERS detection of Hg2+ with a detection limit of 9.56 nM. This work provides guidance for designing ultra-sensitive and highly specific field analysis of heavy metal ions through the modification of CDs' functional groups.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy
Carbon dots
Gold nanoparticles
Hg2+
Nanozyme

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Hg2+ by the formation of Au@HgNPs
Measurement of oxidase-like and peroxidase-like activities
Condition optimization of AuNPs synthesis and SERS detection
The sensitivity, selectivity and accuracy of Hg2+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 and Au@HgNPs (a), Hg2+ detection mechanism (b), and influence of CDs/AuNPs surface groups on Raman signal (c).

The SERS intensity of five CDs/AuNPs-2, CDs/AuNPs-2+MeHg+ and Au@HgNPs system (a); The reducibility of the as-prepared CDs by the KMnO4 fading experiment (b); UV–vis spectra of DTT-CDs, DTT-CDs/AuNPs-1, DTT-CDs/AuNPs-1+Hg2+, DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2, DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2+Hg2+ and DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2+MeHg+ (c); SERS spectra of DTT-CDs + TMB, DTT-CDs/AuNPs-1+TMB and DTT-CDs/AuNPs-1+TMB + Hg2+ (d); The cycle time of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 (e); TEM images of the DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 (f) and Au@HgNPs (g); The percentage of diameter of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 (h) and Au@HgNPs (i); EDX elemental mappings of Au, Hg, C, N, O and S in Au@HgNPs (j).

XRD spectra of the DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 (a); FTIR spectra of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 and Au@HgNPs (b); XPS spectra of the DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 and Au@HgNPs (c); High-resolution XPS spectra of Au (d), Hg (e), C (f), N (g), O (h) and S (i) in Au@HgNPs and DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2.

SERS spectra of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2+TMB interacting with different types of mercury (a); Comparison of the time-dependent catalytic effects of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 on Hg2+ and MeHg+ (b); SERS spectra of TMB (c), TMB + H2O2 (d), LMG (e) and OPD (f) in the presence of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 or Au@HgNPs; The SERS intensity of oxidized TMB by DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 versus time with or without Hg2+ (g–i).

Absorption spectra of different substrates being oxidized (a); UV–vis spectra of TMB + [DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2+Hg2+] with different scavengers (TRP, PBQ and IPA) (b); EPR spectra for the detection of 1O2 (c) and ·O2 − (d) in DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 and Au@HgNPs system; Zeta potential of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 with the addition of HgCl2 (e); The SERS intensity of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 system of different volatilization of solution (f).

head.ce:title: Effects of functional groups on carbon dots-based AuNPs for the specific detection of mercury ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Complex Nonferrous Metal Resources Clean Utilization, Faculty of Metallurgical and Energy Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650093, Yunnan, China

Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Yunce Quality Testing Co., Ltd, Kunming, 650217, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province, 650500, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of highly selective Raman substrates has always been the key to metal ion detection using surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS). Here, a solution volatilization method (SVM) by reducing carbon dots (CDs) was proposed to prepare highly selective gold nanoparticle (AuNPs) as Raman substrates for the specific detection of Hg2+ through SERS. Five CDs-based AuNPs were synthesized by changing the group on the precursor materials for CDs preparation and the secondary reduction with seed method. The results revealed that thiol and hydroxyl groups played a pivotal role in the detection of Hg2+. Interestingly, the influence of the chirality, number and type of the groups on the detection of Hg2+ is substantial. The DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2, prepared from dithiothreitol (DTT) containing two thiol groups and hydroxyl groups, was capable of selectively facilitating the formation of gold amalgamation (Au@HgNPs) with Hg2+, without being affected by methylmercury. The Au@HgNPs showed obviously enhanced oxidase (OXD)-like activity, which can oxidize TMB (weakly SERS-active) to ox-TMB (strongly SERS-active) with strong Raman signal. This led to a rapid and specific SERS detection of Hg2+ with a detection limit of 9.56 nM. This work provides guidance for designing ultra-sensitive and highly specific field analysis of heavy metal ions through the modification of CDs' functional groups.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury and its compounds are easy to accumulate in environmental soil and water, which poses a huge threat to life and health [1]. Mercury ion, a water-soluble and stable bivalent Hg2+, can cause kidney damage, genetic malformation and nerve damage [2,3]. Therefore, a sensitive, specific and rapid Hg2+ detection method is urgent. Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) is a powerful analytical tool, which can provide rapid and point-of-care testing about metal ion [4,5]. Due to the small Raman scattering cross section of metal ions and the absence of vibration mode, it is difficult to directly detect metal ions through SERS, and the indirect detection of metal ions by Raman substrate is usually required [6]. As a kind of Raman substrate, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have a very broad application prospect in sensing technology [7–9]. Saloni Sharma et al. utilized polyacrylonitrile as the composite matrix and combined gold nanoparticles or N-doped graphene quantum dots as Raman substrates, which enabled rapidly and sensitively detect methylene blue [10,11]. Logan et al. utilized amalgam hotspots formed by gold nanostars and mercury to enhance the SERS signal for the detection of mercury ions [12]. Zhang et al. utilized self-assembled Raman signaling DNA and streptavidin-conjugated gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as novel signaling nanoprobes for the detection of Hg2+ [13]. However, the AuNPs is limited by low activity and poor colloidal stability, leading to application obstacles [14].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon materials with a size less than 10 nm and abundant oxygen-containing groups on the surface, which can be used as excellent electron donors and reducing agents [15,16]. AuNPs synthesized by reducing CDs demonstrated enhanced catalytic activity and stability [17,18]. The surface functional groups of CDs significantly influence their properties [19,20], which in turn affect the characteristics of the CDs-AuNPs composites [21]. By changing the surface groups of CDs, Li et al. [22] achieved selective recognition of the target methylmercury. Liu et al. [23] constructed a CDs-gold nanocluster sensor based on the functionalization of dithiothreitol (DTT) for sensitive detection of Hg2+ in water samples. Gupta et al. [24] decreased the detection limit (LOD) of Hg2+ from 6.8 nM to 18 pM by functionalizing the nitrogen-doped PEG CDs with DTT. These results indicated that the sulfhydryl and hydroxyl groups in dithiothreitol may be helpful for the detection of Hg2+.

Herein, five kinds of reductive CDs were prepared by microwave method with DTT, dithioerythritol (DTE), mercaptoethanol (ME), thioglycolic acid (TGA) and dimercaptobutyric acid (DMSA). Next, the CDs was used to synthesize CDs-modified AuNPs (CDs/AuNPs) by SVM method. Notably, the introduction of Hg2+ could lead to a significantly enhanced OXD-like and POD-like activities of the DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2, which is attributed to the formation of Au@HgNPs. Subsequently, the TMB was oxidized by Au@HgNPs to produce ox-TMB, which presented a stronger Raman signal at 1605 cm−1 (Fig. 1 ). Additional zeta potential analysis and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) were also carried out to reveal the potential mechanism for the remarkable enhancement of the Raman signal.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O) was obtained from Sinopharm chemical reagent Co., Ltd (China). DTT, DTE, ME, TGA, DMSA, mercuric chloride (HgCl2), ethylenediamine (EDA), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt% aqueous solution) were obtained from Aladdin Reagents Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). TMB and N,N,N′,N′-tetramethyl-4,4′-benzylidenedianiline (LMG), and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai Co., Ltd., China). Methylmercury (MeHg+) was purchased from the National Center for Reference Materials (Beijing, China). Terephthalic acid and tetrazolium blue (NBT) were purchased from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. All the reagents were of analytical grade. Deionized water was acquired through a Milli-Q water purification system (18 MΩ cm).

The characterization and the mechanisms research were explored by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI tecnai F20), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha, USA), X-ray polycrystalline diffractometer (XRD, Bruker, Germany), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker Tensor 27, Germany), absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis, TU-1901, Persee, Beijing) and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Bruker EMXPLUS, Germany). The size and charge of CDs/AuNPs were tested with dynamic light scattering (DLS, Malven Zetasizer).

DTT-CDs was prepared by a one-step microwave method: in 30 mL deionized water, 0.1 g DL-dithiothreitol, 2.1 g citric acid (CA) monohydrate and 50 μL EDA were dissolved. The ultrasound-assisted transparent solution was formed and transferred to the Teflon microwave vessel. Under 1000 W microwave radiation, it was heated to 180 °C within 1 min and reacted for 60 min. After being cooled to room temperature, the product was filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane. Eventually, the resulting solution was kept at 4 °C for further experiments.

DTT-CDs/AuNPs-1 was synthesized by SVM (In brief, the formation and enlargement of gold nanoparticles is facilitated by evaporating a part of the solution through heating): 30 mL deionized water was added into 100 mL flask. Then, 200 μL HAuCl4, 30 μL DTT-CDs and 50 μL PVP (1 %) were added, heated and stirred for 15 min. Subsequently, the resulting solution was promptly cooled in an ice bath. Finally, the cooled liquid is DTT-CDs/AuNPs-1. For the synthesis of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2, the above solution is not cooled, and the secondary carbon dots reduction with seed method was carried out: 30 μL DTT-CDs and 200 μL HAuCl4 were added, continually heated and stirred for 20 min. DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 was obtained after the ice bath.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ by the formation of Au@HgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: In 96-well plates, different amounts of Hg2+ (0.75–100 μL, 1 mg/L) were added to 50 μL of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2. The volume was adjusted to 250 μL using deionized water, and then incubated for 5 min. Next, the mixtures were incubated for 3 min following the addition of 10 μL of TMB. Finally, Raman spectra of the solutions were recorded for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of oxidase-like and peroxidase-like activities
ce:section.ce:para: Chromogenic reactions was used to evaluate the OXD-like and POD-like activities, but the chromogenic substrate was TMB and TMB + H2O2, respectively. A reaction mixture containing 50 μL of 10 mM TMB (or TMB+50 μL of 200 mM H2O2) and 200 μL of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 or Au@HgNPs was prepared. The variation in absorbance values for the DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 and Au@HgNPs was monitored at a wavelength of 654 nm using the UV–vis spectrometer.

As a kind of Raman substrate, precious metal nanoparticles can greatly amplify SERS signal [25]. In agreement with previous literature reports, the DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 exhibits limited enzymatic and Raman activities, which can facilitate the reduction of Hg2+ to Hg0, resulting in the formation of Au@HgNPs [26,27]. In this study, CDs can be used as both a reducing agent and a stabilizer to generate gold nanoparticles. Interestingly, CDs is also modified on the surface of gold nanoparticles, resulting in high Raman selectivity. The surface groups of CDs have a significant impact on the formation of Au@HgNPs.

To study the effect of the functional groups of DTT on the recognition of Hg2+, four precursor material with similar structure and functional groups, including DTE, ME, TGA and DMSA, were also used to prepare reducing CDs (namely DTE-CDs, ME-CDs, TGA-CD and DMSA-CDs) under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the Raman signals of AuNPs + TMB (i.e. blank group) and AuNPs + TMB + Hg2+ indicate that under the same Hg2+ content, DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 has the strongest enhancement of SERS signal at 1605 cm−1, followed by ME-CDs/AuNPs-2 and TGA-CDs/AuNPs-2. DTE-CDs/AuNPs-2 and DMSA-CDs/AuNPs-2 also have similarly phenomenon. These results showed that although there is only one hydroxyl orientation difference between DTT and DTE, Hg2+ has a significant effect on Raman signal enhancement of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2+TMB and DTE-CDs/AuNPs-2+TMB. ME-CDs/AuNPs-2 synthesized by ME with the same group as DTT showed similar effects. After the hydroxyl group in ME is replaced by the carboxyl group (i.e. TGA), the Raman signal enhancement effect of Hg2+ on the system is similar. It indicated that hydroxyl group and carboxyl group (contains the hydroxyl group) have the same effect on the detection of Hg2+. DMSA contains sulfhydryl and carboxyl groups, but the position structure is different from DTT, resulting in only a weak enhancement of the Raman signal of DMSA-CDs/AuNPs-2+TMB system even after the addition of Hg2+. These results indicated that the position structure of functional groups in precursor material for CDs preparation has great influence on the detection of Hg2+. In addition, the KMnO4 fading experiment was used to evaluate CDs reducibility. As shown in Fig. 2b, the reducing order is DTT-CDs > ME-CDs > DTE-CDs > TGA-CDs > DMSA-CDs, indicating that DTT-CDs is more conducive to reducing Hg2+ to Hg0.

Importantly, it was found that DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 can only reduce Hg2+ to Hg0 even in the presence of MeHg+ and Hg(NO3)2 but not MeHg+ and Hg(NO3)2 to Hg0, demonstrating excellent selectivity for Hg2+ determination. In addition, the formation of Au@HgNPs significantly stimulated and promoted the properties of the AuNPs to OXD-like and POD-like activities. The enhanced OXD-like and POD-like activities could accelerate the oxidation rate of TMB to ox-TMB with SERS active. Therefore, the formation of Au@HgNPs led to a further enhancement of SERS signal. With the increase of Hg2+ concentration, the SERS signal (at 1186, 1332, 1402, and 1605 cm−1) was enhanced, so a new SERS sensor Hg2+ was designed.

There is no obvious change between the UV–vis spectra of DTT-CDs/AuNPs and DTT-CDs/AuNPs + Hg2+ (after incubation) (Fig. 2c). This phenomenon may be attributed to the low concentration of added MeHg+/Hg2+, such that even if a gold amalgam is formed, it is insufficient to significantly alter its absorbance. As shown in Fig. 2d, DTT-CDs/AuNPs-1 shows no enhancement in OXD-like activity and SERS signal in the presence of Hg2+. To evaluate the reproducibility of the synthesis, the UV absorption spectra of six independently synthesized DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 samples were measured (Fig. 2e). The results showed that the synthesis of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 has good reproducibility. DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 showed irregular spherical particles (Fig. 2f). After incubation with Hg2+, DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 did not form large particle aggregates (Fig. 2g), but the average particle size increased (Fig. 2h–i), which is in accordance with the measurement results of DLS, indicating the formation of amalgam. High-resolution TEM images of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 and Au@HgNPs indicated that the lattice spacing at 0.232 nm and 0.230 nm can be attributed to the (111) plane of Au [28,29], and the lattice spacing at 0.205 nm in DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 is consistent with the (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphitic carbon [30].

The presence of C, N, O, S, Au, and Hg elements in the DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 and Au@HgNPs was further revealed by EDX elemental spectra [31]. As shown in Fig. 2j, a uniform distribution of mercury on DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 can be observed, indicating the formation of amalgam. XRD patterns (Fig. 3 a) demonstrate that Au (111) crystal plane is the main crystal plane of AuNPs [32], which is consistent with HRTEM images. The peaks at 3396 cm−1, 2518 cm−1,1396 cm−1, 1209 cm−1, 1071 cm−1, and 572 cm−1 in FT-IR [33] (Fig. 3b) signified the existence of OH, S–H [34], C–H, C–OH, and C–C stretching vibrations, respectively. These results proved that the surface of prepared DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 contains –OH and –SH groups, which further indicates that DTT-CDs is also modified on the surface of gold nanoparticles during the reduction process.

To further verify the role of Hg2+, the surface composition of the DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 substrate before and after reaction with Hg2+ were analyzed by performing XPS (Fig. 3c). As the responses to C, N, O, Au and S elements, DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 have five typical characteristic peaks at 284.6 eV, 401.4 eV, 532.8 eV, 83.6 eV and 168.6 eV. The typical characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV, 401.6 eV, 532.7 eV, 83.7 eV, 168.7 eV and 99.5 eV after the addition of mercury ions are attributed to C, N, O, Au, S and Hg, respectively [35].

The high-resolution XPS spectra of Au 4f, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, S 2p and Hg 4f were fitted. The XPS peaks of Au 4f have two characteristic signals (Fig. 3d): the signals of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 are located at 83.6 eV and 87.2 eV, and the signals of Au@HgNPs are located at 83.7 eV and 87.4 eV, corresponding to Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 of metal Au (0), respectively. After the addition of Hg2+, the binding energy increases by 0.1 eV and 0.2 eV, respectively, indicating an enhanced degree of electron loss [36,37]. These results showed that the addition of Hg2+ results in changes in the binding energy and promotes a stronger catalytic reaction. Meanwhile, after the formation of Au@HgNPs through the reaction between Hg0 and DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2, two high-intensity peaks corresponding to Hg 4f were observed (Fig. 3e) [38]. The binding energies of C, N and O elements also changed after the addition of Hg2+. For DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2, the XPS peak of C 1s can be observed at 284.6 eV, 285.6 eV, and 288.5 eV. For Au@HgNPs, the XPS peak of C 1s can be observed at 284.8, 285.8, and 288.6 eV (Fig. 3f). These C 1s peaks can be attributed to the C–C/CC, C–N, and C–S bonds.

The N 1s spectra exhibit two sets of double peaks. The binding energy values for DTT-CD/AuNPs are 399.8 eV and 401.4 eV, while these values shift to 399.8 eV and 401.6 eV after the addition of Hg2+(Fig. 3g). These peaks can be attributed to pyrrole N and quaternary N configurations, respectively [39]. For DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2, the O 1s spectra exhibit two peaks at 531.6 eV and 532.8 eV (Fig. 3h). Upon the addition of Hg2+, the binding energies decrease to 531.5 eV and 532.7 eV, respectively, indicating an increase in electron cloud density. The S 2p spectra (Fig. 3i) reveal that the peaks at 163.4 eV and 168.7 eV are attributed to the binding energy of the C–S–C bond [40]. Furthermore, the binding energy at 163.8 eV in the S 2p spectra also reveal the existence of H–S groups [34].

According to our previous studies [22], the presence of different forms of mercury may significantly influence the enzyme activity of AuNPs and their Raman signal. Therefore, the interaction of HgCl2, MeHg+ and Hg(NO3)2 with DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 is carefully investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 4 a, in the DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2+TMB group, only the addition of HgCl2 can enhance the Raman signal, whereas Hg(NO3)2 and MeHg + either decrease or have minimal effect on the Raman signal. Hg2+ can be reduced in a chemical path of Hg(II) → Hg(I) → Hg(0) via anti-Galvanic reaction (ARG). Although both HgCl2 and Hg(NO3)2 are divalent mercury, the AGR of Hg2+ derived from different precursors on the surface of Au is different. The formation of Hg2Cl2, which is energetically favorable, occurs first on the Au surface when HgCl2 is present [41]. The insoluble Hg2Cl2 adsorbs onto the Au surface and further forms Hg(0). In contrast, when Hg(NO3)2 is present, the first intermediate formed on the Au surface is the soluble Hg2(NO3)2, which then generates Hg(0) [41]. Due to these different intermediates, resulting in the specific recognition of HgCl2 by DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2. Fig. S1 shows the introduction of Hg2(NO3)2 has little effect on the Raman signal of the system. However, when Cl− are added to Hg2(NO3)2, a significantly enhanced Raman signal is observed, which is consistent with the signal enhancement effect caused by HgCl2. As shown in Fig. 4b, MeHg+ cannot be reduced to Hg0 by DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 within 20 min. The results demonstrated that the formation of Au@HgNPs is not affected by MeHg+. The selective reduction of Hg2+ offers the possibility of constructing a specific detection of Hg2+ based on variations in Raman signal.

TMB, TMB + H2O2, and OPD were used as the substrates to estimate the OXD-like and POD-like activities, as well as the changes in SERS signals. As shown in Fig. 4c–f and Fig. S2, TMB, TMB + H2O2, LMG and OPD are oxidized by DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 and DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2+Hg2+ to produce corresponding oxidation products, which have obvious absorption peaks at 654 nm, 619 nm and 521 nm, respectively [42,43]. Compared with DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2, Au@HgNPs showed superior catalytic activity and a notable SERS enhancement effect, resulting in a stronger SERS signal at 1605 cm-1. The results showed that Au@HgNPs had POD-like and OXD-like activities along with good SERS-activity, and this could be further used in the SERS sensor for Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 5 a, Au@HgNPs has higher enzymatic activity than DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2. Fig. 4g–i shows the time variation of the Raman signal at 1605 cm-1, which was studied by the oxidation of TMB with or without the addition of Hg2+. The results showed that the addition of Hg2+ can accelerate the oxidation rate of TMB, and further enhance the Raman signal of TMB oxidation. At the same time, with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, the Raman signals at 1186 cm-1, 1322 cm-1, 1402 cm-1 and 1605 cm-1 are observed to exhibit an increase. This results further substantiated the application of SERS technique in the detection of the enzyme reaction of TMB, thereby enabling the quantitative analysis of Hg2+.

Meanwhile, the AuNPs without CDs was also synthesized by using conventional reducing agents (i.e. sodium citrate), which was named as AuNPs-sodium citrate. As shown in Fig. S3, the AuNPs-sodium citrate cannot interact with Hg2+ and further enhance OXD-like activity, which indicates that DTT-CDs play a very important role in the interaction between DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 and Hg2+. Furthermore, when AuNPs-sodium citrate was incubated with DTT (rather than DTT-CDs), the OXD-like activity of AuNPs-sodium citrate decreased with the increase of DTT concentrations (Fig. S4). When the concentration of DTT gradually increased, the introduction of Hg2+ and MeHg+ could increase the OXD-like activity of AuNPs-sodium citrate (Fig. S4), but could not enhance the Raman signal and achieve selective detection of Hg2+ (Fig. S5). These results proved that the DTT-CDs and its modification play a significant role for the detection of Hg2+.

The mechanism underlying the enzyme-like activity of nanoparticles depends on the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as ·OH, 1O2 and ·O2 −. Isopropanol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (PBQ) and tryptophan (TRP) were used as scavenger to remove ·OH, ·O2 − and 1O2, respectively. The production of ROS during the catalytic reaction was investigated through capture experiments [44]. The absorbance intensity hardly changed when IPA was added to the TMB + [DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2+Hg2+] group (Fig. 5b), indicating that ·OH is not the main reactive species. The absorbance changed most significantly after the addition of TRP, indicating that 1O2 is the predominant reactive species. The addition of PBQ as a ·O2 − scavenger also showed a notable effect, suggesting that in the reaction system, the XOD-like activity of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 plays a major role.

ROS detection in the reaction system was carried out by EPR. As shown in Fig. 5c–d, 1O2 and ·O2 − are implicated in the Au@HgNPs system. Additionally, the formation of Au@HgNPs enhanced the signals of 1O2 and ·O2 − in the system. In the Au@HgNPs group, the peak intensities of 1O2 and ·O2 − were higher than those in the DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 system, indicating that the enhancement of OXD-like activity is due to the addition of Hg2+, which can produce higher concentrations of ROS. As shown in Fig. 5e, the Zeta potential measurement results indicate that the potential of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 and DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2+Hg2+ are −15.9 mV and 16.9 mV, respectively. The results showed that the negatively charged DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 readily combines with the positively charged Hg2+ to form Au@HgNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition optimization of AuNPs synthesis and SERS detection
ce:section.ce:para: Factors influencing SERS detection included pH value, temperature, the quantity of CDs added in AuNPs synthesis and heating time of AuNPs were optimized. In this study, SVM was proposed to study the enzyme-like activity of AuNPs. The heating time of AuNPs was controlled to control the volatilization amount of AuNPs solutions in beakers. The enzyme-like activity and SERS-activity of AuNPs solutions with different volatilization amounts were also studied. In the secondary encapsulation process of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2, the water evaporation from the DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 solution was controlled to 6, 9, 12, 15, and 18 mL, respectively, corresponding to evaporation times of 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 min. As shown in Fig. 5f, the results show that with the increase of volatilization of AuNPs solution (volatilization reached 15 mL), SERS-activity of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 was enhanced, which may be caused by the enhanced OXD-like activity. When the volatility of solution was 12 mL, the SERS signal of the DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2+TMB group was most significantly enhanced by Hg2+.

In the first step of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2, the effect of the amount of reducing agent on the interaction of Hg2+ with DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 was also studied. Fig. S6a-f and Fig. 4i show the optimal pH, temperature, and quantity of DTT-CDs initially added during the synthesis of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2, with optimal conditions being pH 7, 20 °C, and 30 μL, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensitivity, selectivity and accuracy of Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, TMB was used as a reporter probe, while Au@HgNPs served as efficient Raman substrates. With increasing Hg2+ content, the OXD-like activity of DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 was stimulated by Hg2+, leading to the oxidation of TMB to ox-TMB and resulting in stronger SERS signals at 1186 cm−1, 1322 cm−1, 1402 cm−1, and 1605 cm−1 (Fig. S7a–b). At 1605 cm−1, the SERS signal of the blank group increased from 175620 a.u. to 406370 a.u. Within the concentration range of 14.35–1917.25 nM, a linear relationship between SERS intensity and Hg2+ concentration was observed. The regression equation was SERS intensity = 558.3[Hg2+]+187780 (R2 = 0.9909). The limit of detection for this method was determined to be 9.56 nM (3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of the three blank experiments and k represents the slope). As shown in Fig. S8, the relative standard deviation of the SERS intensity of TMB at 1605 cm−1 is 3.20 % (n = 10), indicating that the reproducibility of the data is satisfactory. In the Hg2+ analysis substrates, our Raman substrate demonstrated excellent sensitivity compared to other substrates (see Table S1).

The selectivity of the proposed substrate was also evaluated by adding different ions to Au@HgNPs + TMB group (1000-fold concentration of Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, H2PO4 −, HCO3 −, and SO4 2−). As shown in Fig. S9a–b, the above ions have no effect on the Raman signal of the Au@HgNPs + TMB group. Then, the accuracy was evaluated by adding standard Hg2+ into environmental water samples, the optimized SERS sensor was used to measure the content of Hg2+. As shown in Table S2, the spiking (40, 160, 320 μg L−1) recoveries of Hg2+ were in the range of 95.25–111.40 %. The results demonstrated the Raman substrate not only has excellent sensitivity and selectivity, but also demonstrates remarkable accuracy.

In summary, our study found that thiol and hydroxyl groups play an important role in the SERS detection of Hg2+. Five types of CDs as reducing agents, varying in chirality, number, and type of functional groups, were synthesized and then used to prepare five different CDs-modified AuNPs using proposed SVM method. Importantly, we found that DTT-CDs/AuNPs-2 (which contains two thiol and hydroxyl groups) showed the best OXD-like activity and SERS activity after forming Au@HgNPs with Hg2+. Based on this phenomenon, it is expected to develop novel and controllable Raman substrates. The sensitivity, selectivity and even the target of the substrates can be regulated by chirality, number, and type of functional groups. This work provides further insights into designing Raman substrates and other metal ion sensors.

Shaopeng Xie: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Dezhi Yang: Writing – review & editing, Software, Resources, Funding acquisition. Qiulan Li: Validation, Investigation. Jitao Li: Methodology. Dubo Zhong: Funding acquisition. Ye Yang: Funding acquisition. Yaling Yang: Methodology. Hua Wang: Supervision, Resources.
No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was greatly supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC Grant No. 32302210), Leading Metallurgical Energy-Saving & Emission-Reduction Team of Yunnan Province (No. 202405AS350028), Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (No. 202201AU070117), National Key R&D Plan (No. 2023YFC3503802), Yunnan Sanqi Industry Technology Innovation Center (No. 202305AK340079), and Postdoctoral research project of Yunnan Province.
